blurb: Colorado's most dependable native penstemon — dense wands of deep blue-violet tubular flowers over glossy strap-shaped leaves, a magnet for native bees and hummingbirds that thrives on lean, dry, unimproved soil.
water: Low; xeric once established — no supplemental water in average years
seasons: Blue-violet flower spikes late spring–early summer; semi-evergreen basal rosette; dark seed capsules persist into winter
wildlife: Major nectar source for native bees and bumblebees; visited by hummingbirds
deer: Seldom browsed — deer and rabbit resistant

shots.cap: Summer — flowering drift · © Jacquelyn Fitzgerald (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — flower close-up · © Bryan Guarente (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Foliage & flowering stems · © Erik Danielsen (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: One of the most cold-hardy, easy beardtongues for the Front Range — reliably perennial through Boulder's USDA 5b–6a winters (a semi-evergreen native forb of the southern Rockies, ~5,000–9,000 ft).[2][3]
care.planting: Set out container plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall so roots establish before winter; space into unamended, gritty ground. In pots use a lean, fast-draining cactus/gravel mix — containers stay wetter and rot crowns far more readily than open ground, so never let them sit soggy.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — at least six hours — for erect stems and the clearest blue color; tolerates light afternoon shade but flops and pales in too much.[1]
care.soil: Demands sharp drainage and lean soil; it is the most clay-tolerant of the penstemons but still resents Boulder's heavy, wet ground — plant on a slope, in gritty/decomposed-granite soil, and do NOT enrich with compost, which shortens its life and invites crown rot.[1][2]
care.water: Xeric once established — low water, though it wants adequate spring moisture to bloom well; overwatering after flowering causes crown and root rot, so keep summer irrigation minimal.[1]
care.spacing: Space roughly 12–18 in apart; plants reach about 1.5–3 ft tall in bloom and form an evergreen basal mat.[2][3]
care.propagation: Easiest from seed, which needs cold-moist stratification: fall-sow outdoors for natural chilling, or stratify ~4–6 weeks moist at ~40°F before a late-winter indoor start. Some seed waits until the second year, and plants typically first bloom their second season. Penstemons resent division — propagate clones instead from short basal/softwood cuttings rather than splitting the crown.[2][1]
care.sow: Sow 1/8 to no more than 1/4 in deep into a firm, weed-free seedbed with good seed-to-soil contact; fall sowing supplies the needed natural cold stratification.[2]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify about 4–6 weeks at ~40°F (or fall-sow for natural winter chilling) to break dormancy.[2]
care.bloom: Spikes of deep blue-purple tubular flowers in late spring to early summer (about May–June on the Front Range); deadhead spent stalks to tidy and lengthen plant life.[1][3]
care.feeding: None needed — in ordinary garden soil penstemons require no fertilizer, and feeding them only makes lanky growth and shortens their lifespan.[1]
care.maintenance: Low: deadhead after bloom (but ease off summer water once it does, to avoid crown rot), and leave the evergreen basal rosette over winter; cut spent flower stems back in late fall or early spring.[1]
care.troubles: Few problems beyond crown/root rot in wet, poorly drained or amended soil; grasshoppers, flea beetles and rust fungi are occasional pests.[1]
care.companions: A workhorse for xeric beds and pollinator borders — pairs with other dryland natives and ornamental grasses in lean, sunny ground.[2]

references.name: USDA NRCS Plant Guide — Rocky Mountain penstemon (Penstemon strictus)
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Penstemon strictus

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally as a native pollinator plant — no documented edible use.[3]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[3]
